question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).credit and reverts if credit is zero (maker has no credit), under PRECONDITIONS where the maker is a new lender with no existing credit, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: new lender creates offer -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads credit -> zero -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a new lender must be able to have their first offer filled, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for new lenders with no existing credit? Proof idea: deploy zero-credit-checking ratifier; assert take reverts for new lenders.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, taker).debt and reverts if taker debt is above a threshold, under PRECONDITIONS where the taker (borrower) already has high debt, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker accumulates debt -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads debtOf(id, taker) -> above threshold -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable regardless of the taker's existing debt, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for over-leveraged takers? Proof idea: accumulate taker debt; deploy taker-debt-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).debt and reverts if maker debt is nonzero (maker is also a borrower), under PRECONDITIONS where the maker has both credit and debt (which is impossible per the invariant that a user cannot hold both), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: ratifier reads maker debt -> nonzero -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a maker with debt cannot also have credit (protocol invariant), causing scoped impact: DoS on take if the credit-debt mutual exclusion invariant is violated? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test asserting position.credit > 0 => position.debt == 0 for all users after any sequence of calls.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).lastAccrual and reverts if lastAccrual is not equal to block.timestamp, combined with a requirement that the maker must call updatePosition in the same block as any take, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and the maker has not interacted in the current block, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: time passes -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads lastAccrual -> stale -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable without requiring same-block maker interaction, causing scoped impact: DoS on take unless maker updates position every block? Proof idea: deploy lastAccrual-staleness ratifier; assert take reverts unless maker called updatePosition in same block.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).collateral[0] and reverts if it is zero, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker has no collateral at index 0, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker has no collateral at index 0 -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads collateral[0] -> zero -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a lender (buy offer maker) must be able to have their offer filled without having collateral, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for lenders with no collateral? Proof idea: deploy collateral-zero-checking ratifier; assert take reverts for lenders.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.isAuthorized(maker, maker) (self-authorization) and reverts if it returns false, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker has not self-authorized, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker has not called setIsAuthorized(maker, true, maker) -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads isAuthorized(maker, maker) -> false -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a maker must be able to have their offer filled without self-authorizing, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for makers who have not self-authorized? Proof idea: deploy self-auth-checking ratifier; assert take reverts for makers without self-authorization.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).credit and Midnight.position(id, maker).pendingFee and reverts if credit - pendingFee is below a threshold (i.e., net credit is too low), under PRECONDITIONS where the maker's net credit is low due to high pending fees, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker accumulates high pendingFee -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads credit and pendingFee -> net credit below threshold -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable regardless of the maker's net credit position, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for makers with high pending fees relative to credit? Proof idea: accumulate high pendingFee; deploy net-credit-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.marketState(id).totalUnits and Midnight.marketState(id).withdrawable and reverts if totalUnits - withdrawable is zero (no outstanding debt), under PRECONDITIONS where all debt has been repaid, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: all debt repaid -> totalUnits == withdrawable -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads totalUnits and withdrawable -> equal -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable even in a fully repaid market, causing scoped impact: DoS on take in fully repaid markets? Proof idea: repay all debt; deploy zero-debt-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).credit and Midnight.marketState(id).totalUnits and reverts if maker credit / totalUnits > 0.5 (maker holds more than half the market), under PRECONDITIONS where the maker is the dominant lender, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker accumulates dominant credit -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads credit and totalUnits -> ratio > 0.5 -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a dominant lender must still be able to have their offers filled, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for dominant lenders? Proof idea: deploy market-share-checking ratifier; assert take reverts for dominant lenders.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).credit and Midnight.position(id, maker).pendingFee and computes a mulDivDown that overflows, causing a revert, under PRECONDITIONS where credit and pendingFee are at their maximum values (type(uint128).max), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker has max credit and pendingFee -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier computes mulDivDown(credit, pendingFee, WAD) -> overflow -> revert -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid take must not revert due to arithmetic overflow in the ratifier, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for makers with max credit/fee values? Proof idea: fuzz credit and pendingFee near type(uint128).max; deploy overflow-prone ratifier; assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.position(id, maker).credit and Midnight.marketState(id).continuousFeeCredit and reverts if continuousFeeCredit > credit (fee credit exceeds individual credit), under PRECONDITIONS where the market has accumulated more fee credit than the maker's individual credit, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: fee credit accumulates -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads continuousFeeCredit and maker credit -> fee credit > maker credit -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable regardless of the relationship between market-level and user-level fee accounting, causing scoped impact: DoS on take in high-fee markets? Proof idea: accumulate high continuousFeeCredit; deploy fee-ratio-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.
